Chen Zhen (Xiaoqi)
陳震 (孝起)
Lived AD ?–235

Chen Zhen, styledXiaoqi, was fromNanyang[南陽].
When theFormer Lordwas leading asGovernorofJing province[荊州牧], he [Chen Zhen] was recruited to be aClerk, supervising the various commanderies. He followed theFormer Lordinto Shu [蜀]. When Shu [蜀] was stabilized, he served asChief Commandantof North Sector ofShu commandery[蜀郡北部都尉]. When the commandery name was changed, he becameGrand AdministratorofWenshan[汶山太守]. He was transferred to be inJianwei[犍為].
In the third year of theera [225], he entered to be conferred asSecretariat Minister. He was promoted to becomeDirector of the Secretariat. He undertook orders to become an envoy toWu.
In the seventh year [229],Sun Quanclaimed the imperial title.[Chen] Zhenwas madeMinister of the Guardsand he congratulated[Sun] Quanon his treading of the throne steps.Zhuge Liangwrote a letter to his older brother,[Zhuge] Jin, stating:
“Xiaoqihas a loyal and pure personality, and as he ages, he grows more sincere. His praises for theeastandwest[relationship] makes it one of merriness and unity! He is quite precious!”
When[Chen] Zhenwas entering the borders ofWu, he sent this message to the watchtowers:
“Theeastandwesthave sent messengers by means of post horses to and fro, so much so that carriages can see the canopies of each other. As for our earlier alliance, we are renewing it with each day. Theimperial easthas received sacred blessings, burned firewood as an communication,1and received [imperial] portents. We shall partition the whole land, and the realm under heaven shall respond to us, with each of us having our respective submissions. At this time, we should suppress thetraitorswith the selfsame heart. How could our enemies not be vanquished then? Thelordand subjects of thewest courteagerly cranes their necks and would be fain to rely on such. I am not talented, yet I fill the role of an envoy. I undertook [orders] to visit as an envoy to discuss our alliance. When I treaded the boundary, I frolicked; entering it was like returning [home]! When theAstuteMaster [of Fan] [范獻子] visited Lu [魯], he violated the mountain taboo.2TheChroniclesridiculed him for that. I hope you shall tell me [of your taboos] so as to have our travelers be in harmony with them. Today, I am unfurling my standards to warn the soldiers that each of them must uphold the oath. I shall travel downstream rapidly, but because the laws of our nations have different rules, I fear I may commit some misdeed. I hope you shall thoughtfully instruct me to show me what is deemedappropriate.”•••
Emperors would burn firewood at an altar to sacrifice to Heaven, thus “communicating” with it.↩
In Lu, there were two mountains that shared the names of some of its past rulers. He broke the “mountain taboo” by saying the mountain names, thus disrespecting the past rulers.↩
When[Chen] Zhenarrived inWuchang[武昌],Sun Quanand[Chen] Zhenascended an altar to smear blood on themselves, swearing an oath to partition the world:Xu[徐],Yu[豫],You[幽], andQing[青] were placed underWuwhileBing[并],Liang[涼],Ji[冀], andYan[兖] were placed underShu. Of the lands of thecentral province,Hangu Pass[函谷關] was made the border [between the two states]. When[Chen] Zhenreturned, he was enfeoffed asDistrict Marquisof Chengyang [城陽亭侯].
In the ninth year [231],Chief ProtectorLi Pingwas implicated of slander and deception, so he was deposed.Zhuge Liangsent a letter toChief ClerkJiang WanandPalace AttendantDong Yun. It stated:
“In the past, beforeXiaoqileft to arrive inWu, he told me thatZhengfangharbored treachery in his heart3and that his own fellow villagers believed he was someone not to be close with. I thought that treacherous people need only not be infringed upon and nothing more.4I did not anticipate him to repeat the deeds ofSu [Qin]andZhang [Yi],5this was beyond the unexpected! Do haveXiaoqiknow ofthis.”•••
The word “treachery” here is from the Chinese metaphor “scaly armor” [鱗甲]. “Heart” [腹] can also be read as “stomach” or “belly”, so an alternative translation of this phrase would be “Zhengfanghad scaly armor in his stomach”, but I thought it would read awkwardly, and so I chose to simplify it.↩
Zhuge Liangpresumably thinks that leaving alone treacherous people would give them no reason to rebel, and that they would correct themselves without any outside action. If this is indeed his thought process,Zhuge Liangmay have been following the Daoist principle of “non-action” [無為].↩
Su QinandZhang Yiwere brilliant strategists, but they were notorious for using deception to influence the rulers of the warring states, as well as shifting allegiances between different states.↩
In the thirteenth year [235],[Chen] Zhendied. His son,[Chen] Ji, succeeded him.

Chen Zhen (Xiaoqi)

Chen Zhen, styled Xiaoqi, was a man from Nanyang. When the First Sovereign [Liu Bei] took over the position of Governor of Jing province, Chen Zhen was hired as a local officer and was stationed in various commanderies. He then followed the First Sovereign into Shu. Once Shu was secured, he was made Chief Commandant of the Northern Region; later, when the northern region was renamed Wenshan Commandery, Chen Zhen was made its Grand Administrator. Afterwards, he was transferred to Qianwei commandery. In the third year of Jianxing (AD 225), he was summoned to the capital as an Imperial Secretariat, and in time was promoted to Head of the Imperial Secretarial Office and sent as an envoy to Wu.
In the seventh year (AD 229), Sun Quan declared himself emperor. Chen Zhen was made Commandant of the Palace Guard1and sent to congratulate Quan’s assumption of the throne. Zhuge Liang wrote thus to his elder brother, Zhuge Jin, “The pure and loyal character of Xiaoqi becomes even better with age. He contribution is most invaluable, in praising both the East and the West, causing the two sides to be joyfully at peacetogether.”•••
One of the Nine Ministers.↩
When Chen Zhen crossed the border into Wu, he wrote an official document to the officers at the border. It said, “Between the East and the West emissaries travel unceasingly back and forth, and ever can the carriage of one see another on the road. Day by day we make progress in renewing our friendship and alliance with each other. The Lord of the East, having received the Sacred Mandate, has accordingly performed the ritual sacrifices and thus granted the imperial title. [Together our two countries shall] divide up the earthly realm, rallying all under Heaven to our cause, and the people [of each land] shall know their lord. At this time, if we cooperate to fight the evildoers,2what enemy would we encounter and not vanquish? Lord and vassal alike in the Western court look to you now, hearts gladdened at having such a reliableally.•••
Implying Wei.↩
Though possessing no talent to speak of, I, Chen Zhen, have the good fortune to serve as a lowly emissary to visit and to articulate our friendship. Since I passed into your lands, I have rejoiced—for [the hospitality is such that] I feel as if I am at home. When Xianzi went on a mission to Lu state, he violated the taboo [on the name of the lord of Lu] when asking of the name of a mountain, and he was ridiculed by the Spring and Autumn Annals for that. I hope you will let us know [the taboos of your kingdom], so that we travellers may remain in peace with you. On this day I will announce with banners to my subordinates that each man should behave responsibly as part of our alliance. We now travel swiftly down the river; as kingdoms vary in their laws and ordinances, I worry that we would violate [your laws] by chance, and thus pray that you may instruct us and let us know what the proper ways are.”
Once Chen Zhen arrived at Wuchang, Sun Quan and him went up to the altar together, and swore an oath of alliance and of divvying up the world: Xu, Yu, You, and Qing provinces would belong to Wu, while Bing, Liang, Ji, and Yan would go to Shu. The land of Sili3was split along the Han’gu Pass.•••
The central administrative region including the city of Luoyang.↩
Upon his return, Chen Zhen was made Marquis of Chengyang Commune. In the ninth year (AD 231), Chief Commissioner Li Ping4was dismissed due to his supplying the court with false information. Zhuge Liang wrote thus to Chief Clerk Jiang Wan and Privy Counsellor Dong Yun, “Right before Xiaoqi last went to Wu, he told me that Zhengfang5seemed like one who has scales over his bosom—even his friends and neighbours find him difficult to get close to. I had thought that one with scales should be alright as long as he is left alone, but I did not expect him to be like Su Qin and Zhang Yi6in his deception. Let Xiaoqi know about this.” In the thirteenth year (AD 235), Chen Zhen died. His son Chen Ji succeededhim.•••
i.e., Li Yan.↩
Li Yan’s style name.↩
Su Qin and Zhang Yi were politician-strategists of the late Warring State era—one advised the powerful Qin state in the taking of the other six states, and the other lobbied the six states to join forces to repel Qin. Zhuge Liang here is comparing Li Yan/Ping’s using deceptive words for his own purposes to Su and Zhang’s using words to control the rulers of the warring states.↩
